---
title: "Opening Ceremony Austie"
---

to do

-   read the JOhn egan report

-   adapt portlaw ceremony and weave it in with dungarvan

-   john egan aftermath

-   call ?Tommy Mooney about the Waterford Brigades

He lay in the bath soaking up the enormity of the day, of the situation, of the life had made for himself and the livelihoods he had created. How many children went to bed today with a full belly on account of his factory? There must be hundreds. Ken always said that. Employ family men because then you feed more mouths and do more good.

The whole town had come out to cheer them on. It was like St Patrick's Day only bigger and louder. Minister Sean Lemass had come down for the jamboree, just as he had in Portlaw in '35 when Austie was an onlooker for the day. It was good to see the set up that day and to compare it to here. You had Lemass there at both, and the Bishop and the parish priest, the directors and **get the list of attendees at portlaw opening versus dickens**

But Portlaw was hardly a town. They had to drive in the food from Waterford because there was no hotel in the town to cook the dinner for the dozens of people

Dungarvan was a great place to be and the factory was exactly what it needed. A proper town with jobs for all. Up to now, you either had a family business in the town or you had work on the farms nearby. There was very little for the young fellas walking round or standing on street corners with nothing to do.
